title: American football

infobox.value: [International Federation of American Football](/wiki/International_Federation_of_American_Football "International Federation of American Football")

[November 6, 1869](/wiki/1869_Princeton_vs._Rutgers_football_game "1869 Princeton vs. Rutgers football game") [New Brunswick, New Jersey](/wiki/New_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "New Brunswick, New Jersey"), U.S. ([Princeton](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers_football "Princeton Tigers football") vs. [Rutgers](/wiki/Rutgers_Scarlet_Knights_football "Rutgers Scarlet Knights football"))
11 (both teams may [freely substitute](/wiki/Free_substitution "Free substitution") players between downs)

* [Football](/wiki/Ball_(gridiron_football) "Ball (gridiron football)") * [Football helmet](/wiki/Football_helmet "Football helmet") * [Pads](/wiki/Padding "Padding") ([shoulder](/wiki/Shoulder_pads_(sport) "Shoulder pads (sport)"), thigh, [knee](/wiki/Knee_pads "Knee pads"), and optionally [elbow pads](/wiki/Elbow_pads "Elbow pads"))

[Demonstrated](/wiki/Demonstration_sport "Demonstration sport") at the [1904](/wiki/American_football_at_the_1904_Summer_Olympics "American football at the 1904 Summer Olympics") and [1932 Summer Olympics](/wiki/American_football_at_the_1932_Summer_Olympics "American football at the 1932 Summer Olympics"), [flag football](/wiki/Flag_football "Flag football") at the [2028 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2028_Summer_Olympics "2028 Summer Olympics")

Invitational sport in [2005](/wiki/American_football_at_the_2005_World_Games "American football at the 2005 World Games"), [2017](/wiki/American_football_at_the_2017_World_Games "American football at the 2017 World Games"); official (flag football) in [2022](/wiki/Flag_football_at_the_2022_World_Games "Flag football at the 2022 World Games"), [2025](/wiki/Flag_football_at_the_2025_World_Games "Flag football at the 2025 World Games")

infobox.value: | * [Early history](/wiki/Early_history_of_American_football "Early history of American football") * [Modern history](/wiki/Modern_history_of_American_football "Modern history of American football") * [First game](/wiki/1869_Princeton_vs._Rutgers_football_game "1869 Princeton vs. Rutgers football game") * [First pro league](/wiki/National_Football_League_(1902) "National Football League (1902)") * [First pro player](/wiki/Pudge_Heffelfinger "Pudge Heffelfinger") * [Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp")

| * [Australian](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football") * [Boston game](/wiki/Boston_game "Boston game") * [Canadian](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football") * [Medieval](/wiki/Medieval_football "Medieval football") * [Old division](/wiki/Old_division_football "Old division football") * [Rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") * [Rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") * [Soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football")

| * [Black players in professional football](/wiki/Black_players_in_professional_American_football "Black players in professional American football") * [Canadian football comparison](/wiki/Comparison_of_American_and_Canadian_football "Comparison of American and Canadian football") * [Concussions](/wiki/Concussions_in_American_football "Concussions in American football") * [Glossary](/wiki/Glossary_of_American_football_terms "Glossary of American football terms") * [Hall of Fame (College)](/wiki/College_Football_Hall_of_Fame "College Football Hall of Fame") * [Hall of Fame (Pro)](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") * [Homosexuality](/wiki/Homosexuality_in_American_football "Homosexuality in American football") * [Rugby league comparison](/wiki/Comparison_of_American_football_and_rugby_league "Comparison of American football and rugby league") * [Rugby union comparison](/wiki/Comparison_of_American_football_and_rugby_union "Compa

in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") and also known as [gridiron football](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football"),[[nb 1]](#cite_note-4) is a [team sport](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") played by two teams of eleven players on a rectangular [field](/wiki/American_football_field "American football field") with goalposts at each end. The [offense](/wiki/Offense_(sports) "Offense (sports)"), the team with possession of the oval-shaped [football](/wiki/Ball_(gridiron_football) "Ball (gridiron football)"), attempts to advance down the field by [running with the ball](/wiki/Rush_(gridiron_football) "Rush (gridiron football)") or [throwing it](/wiki/Forward_pass#Gridiron_football "Forward pass"), while the [defense](/wiki/Defense_(sports) "Defense (sports)"), the team without possession of the ball, aims to stop the offense's advance and to take control of the ball for themselves. The offense must advance the ball at least ten [yards](/wiki/Yard "Yard") in four [downs](/wiki/Down_(gridiron_football) "Down (gridiron football)") or plays; if they fail, they [turn over](/wiki/Turnover_on_downs "Turnover on downs") the football to the defense. If they succeed, they are given a new set of four downs to continue the [drive](/wiki/Glossary_of_American_football#drive "Glossary of American football"). Points are scored primarily by advancing the ball into the opposing team's [end zone](/wiki/End_zone "End zone") for a [touchdown](/wiki/Touchdown "Touchdown") or kicking the ball through the opponent's goalposts for a [field goal](/wiki/Field_goal "Field goal"). The team with the most points at the end of the game wins. In the case of a tie after four quarters, the game enters [overtime](/wiki/Overtime_(sports)#American_and_Canadian_football "Overtime (sports)").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Team field sport

This article is about the American variation of gridiron football. For other uses, see [American football (disambiguation)](/wiki/American_football_(disambiguation) "American football (disambiguation)").

American football

**American football**, referred to simply as **football** in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") and also known as [gridiron football](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football"),[[nb 1]](#cite_note-4) is a [team sport](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") played by two teams of eleven players on a rectangular [field](/wiki/American_football_field "American football field") with goalposts at each end. The [offense](/wiki/Offense_(sports) "Offense (sports)"), the team with possession of the oval-shaped [football](/wiki/Ball_(gridiron_football) "Ball (gridiron football)"), attempts to advance down the field by [running with the ball](/wiki/Rush_(gridiron_football) "Rush (gridiron football)") or [throwing it](/wiki/Forward_pass#Gridiron_football "Forward pass"), while the [defense](/wiki/Defense_(sports) "Defense (sports)"), the team without possession of the ball, aims to stop the offense's advance and to take control of the ball for themselves. The offense must advance the ball at least ten [yards](/wiki/Yard "Yard") in four [downs](/wiki/Down_(gridiron_football) "Down (gridiron football)") or plays; if they fail, they [turn over](/wiki/Turnover_on_downs "Turnover on downs") the football to the defense. If they succeed, they are given a new set of four downs to continue the [drive](/wiki/Glossary_of_American_football#drive "Glossary of American football"). Points are scored primarily by advancing the ball into the opposing team's [end zone](/wiki/End_zone "End zone") for a [touchdown](/wiki/Touchdown "Touchdown") or kicking the ball through the opponent's goalposts for a [field goal](/wiki/Field_goal "Field goal"). The team with the most points at the end of the game wins. In the case of a tie after four quarters, the game enters [overtime](/wiki/Overtime_(sports)#American_and_Canadian_football "Overtime (sports)").

American football evolved in the United States, originating from the sports of [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football"). The first American football game was played on [November 6, 1869](/wiki/1869_New_Jersey_vs._Rutgers_football_game "1869 New Jersey vs. Rutgers football game"), between two college teams, [Rutgers](/wiki/Rutgers_Scarlet_Knights_football "Rutgers Scarlet Knights football") and [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers_football "Princeton Tigers football"), using rules based on the rules of soccer at the time; by 1875, the sport had adopted rules similar to [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"). A set of rule changes drawn up from [1880](/wiki/1880_college_football_season "1880 college football season") onward by [Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp"), the "Father of American Football", established concepts that would define the sport, among them the [snap](/wiki/Snap_(gridiron_football) "Snap (gridiron football)"), the [line of scrimmage](/wiki/Line_of_scrimmage "Line of scrimmage"), eleven-player teams, and the concept of downs. Later rule changes legalized the [forward pass](/wiki/Forward_pass "Forward pass"), created the [neutral zone](/wiki/Neutral_zone_(gridiron_football) "Neutral zone (gridiron football)"), and specified the size and shape of the football. The modern sport [is closely related](/wiki/Comparison_of_American_and_Canadian_football "Comparison of American and Canadian football") to [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football"), which evolved in parallel with and at the same time as the American game; the two sports are considered the primary variants of [gridiron football](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football").

American football is the most popular [sport in the United States](/wiki/Sport_in_the_United_States "Sport in the United States"); the most popular forms of the game are [professional](/wiki/Professional_football_(gridiron) "Professional football (gridiron)") and [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football"), with the other major levels being [high school](/wiki/High_school_football "High school football") and youth football. Over a million Americans played college or high school football in 2022, and the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) has one of the [highest average attendance of any professional sports league in the world](/wiki/List_of_sports_attendance_figures#Top_leagues_in_average_attendance "List of sports attendance figures"). Its championship game, the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), ranks among the most-watched club sporting events globally. Other professional and amateur leagues exist worldwide, but the sport does not have the international popularity of other American sports like [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") or [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"); the sport maintains a growing following in the rest of North America, Europe, [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). [Flag football](/wiki/Flag_football "Flag football"), a variant of the sport, will be played at the [2028 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2028_Summer_Olympics "2028 Summer Olympics").

## Etymology and names

Main article: [*Football* (word)](/wiki/Football_(word) "Football (word)")

In the United States, American football is referred to as "football".[[4]](#cite_note-Football_Or_Soccer?_What's_In_A_Name?-5) The term "football" was officially established in the rulebook for the [1876 college football season](/wiki/1876_college_football_season "1876 college football season"), when the sport first shifted from soccer-style rules to rugby-style rules. Although it could easily have been called "rugby" at this point, Harvard, one of the primary proponents of the rugby-style game, compromised and did not request the name of the sport be changed to "rugby".[[5]](#cite_note-6) The terms "[gridiron](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football")" or "American football" are favored in English-speaking countries where other types of [football](/wiki/Football_(word) "Football (word)") are popular, such as the United Kingdom, Ireland, New Zealand, and Australia.[[6]](#cite_note-'In_the_six'_and_football's_other_strange_Americanisms-7)[[7]](#cite_note-Living_off_the_grid:_American_football_in_coastal_Australia-8)

### Early history

Main article: [Early history of American football](/wiki/Early_history_of_American_football "Early history of American football")

American football evolved from the sports of [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") and [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). Rugby, like American football, is a sport in which two competing teams vie for control of a ball, which can be kicked through a set of goalposts or run into the opponent's goal area to score points.[[8]](#cite_note-The_basics_of_rugby_union-9)

What is considered to be the first American football game was played on [November 6, 1869](/wiki/1869_Princeton_vs._Rutgers_football_game "1869 Princeton vs. Rutgers football game"), between [Rutgers](/wiki/Rutgers_Scarlet_Knights_football "Rutgers Scarlet Knights football") and [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers_football "Princeton Tigers football"), two college teams. They consisted of 25 players per team and used a round ball that could not be picked up or carried. It could, however, be kicked or batted with the feet, hands, head, or sides, with the objective being to advance it into the opponent's goal. Rutgers won the game 6–4.[[9]](#cite_note-Rutgers-10)[[10]](#cite_note-No_Christian_End-11) Collegiate play continued for several years with games played using the rules of the host school. Representatives of [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), Princeton and Rutgers met on October 19, 1873, to create a standard set of rules for use by all schools. Teams were set at 20 players each, and fields of 400 by 250 feet (122 m × 76 m) were specified. Harvard abstained from the conference, as they favored a rugby-style game that allowed running with the ball.[[10]](#cite_note-No_Christian_End-11) After playing [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") using American rules (known as "the [Boston game](/wiki/Boston_game "Boston game")") for the first game and Canadian (rugby) rules for the second one,[[10]](#cite_note-No_Christian_End-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) the [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard "Harvard") players preferred the Canadian style of having only 11 men on the field, running the ball without having to be chased by an opponent, the forward pass, tackling, and using an oblong instead of a round ball.[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14)

An 1875 Harvard–Yale game played under rugby-style rules was observed by two Princeton athletes who were impressed by it. They introduced the sport to Princeton, a feat the [Professional Football Researchers Association](/wiki/Professional_Football_Researchers_Association "Professional Football Researchers Association") compared to "selling [refrigerators](/wiki/Refrigerator "Refrigerator") to [Eskimos](/wiki/Eskimo "Eskimo")".[[10]](#cite_note-No_Christian_End-11) Princeton, Harvard, Yale, and Columbia then agreed to intercollegiate play using a form of [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") rules with a modified scoring system.[[14]](#cite_note-Camp_and_His_Followers-15) These schools formed the [Intercollegiate Football Association](/wiki/Intercollegiate_Football_Association "Intercollegiate Football Association"), although Yale did not join until 1879. Yale player [Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp"), now regarded as the "Father of American Football",[[14]](#cite_note-Camp_and_His_Followers-15)[[15]](#cite_note-NFL1869-16) secured rule changes in 1880 that reduced the size of each team from 15 to 11 players and instituted the [snap](/wiki/Snap_(gridiron_football) "Snap (gridiron football)") to replace the chaotic and inconsistent [scrum](/wiki/Scrum_(rugby) "Scrum (rugby)").[[14]](#cite_note-Camp_and_His_Followers-15) While the game between Rutgers and Princeton is commonly considered the first American football game, several years prior in 1862, the [Oneida Football Club](/wiki/Oneida_Football_Club "Oneida Football Club") formed as the oldest known football club in the United States. The team consisted of graduates of Boston's elite preparatory schools and played from 1862 to 1865.[[16]](#cite_note-Remembering_the_first_high_school_football_games-17)

### Evolution of the game

[![Walter Camp standing by the railing on a bridge](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Walter_Camp_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_18048.jpg/250px-Walter_Camp_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_18048.jpg)](/wiki/File:Walter_Camp_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_18048.jpg)

[Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp"), the "Father of American Football", in 1878 when Camp was captain of [Yale University's football team](/wiki/Yale_Bulldogs_football "Yale Bulldogs football")

[![Cartoon showing a figure with a skeletal head holding a football upright with extended arms while lying down on a football field](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/WCMorris_Spokesman-Review_cartoons_099_%281%29.jpg/250px-WCMorris_Spokesman-Review_cartoons_099_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:WCMorris_Spokesman-Review_cartoons_099_(1).jpg)

The dangers associated with the sport depicted in a 1908 cartoon by [William Charles Morris](/wiki/William_Charles_Morris "William Charles Morris")

The introduction of the snap resulted in an unexpected consequence. Before the snap, the strategy had been to punt if a scrum resulted in a bad field position. However, a group of Princeton players realized that, as the snap was uncontested, they could now hold the ball indefinitely to prevent their opponent from scoring. In 1881, in a game between Yale and Princeton, both teams used this strategy to maintain their undefeated records. Each team held the ball, gaining no ground, for an entire half, resulting in a 0–0 tie. This "block game" proved extremely unpopular with both teams' spectators and fans.[[14]](#cite_note-Camp_and_His_Followers-15)

A rule change was necessary to prevent this strategy from taking hold, and a reversion to the scrum was considered. However, Camp successfully proposed a rule in 1882 that limited each team to three [downs](/wiki/Down_(gridiron_football) "Down (gridiron football)"), or tackles, to advance the ball 5 yards (4.6 m). Failure to advance the ball the required distance within those three downs would result in control of the ball being forfeited to the other team. This change effectively made American football a separate sport from rugby, and the resulting five-yard lines added to the field to measure distances made it resemble a [gridiron](/wiki/Gridiron_(cooking) "Gridiron (cooking)") in appearance. Other major rule changes included a reduction of the field size to 110 by 53+1⁄3 yards (100.6 m × 48.8 m) and the adoption of a scoring system that awarded four points for a touchdown, two for a safety and a [goal following a touchdown](/wiki/Conversion_(gridiron_football) "Conversion (gridiron football)"), and five for a [goal from the field](/wiki/Field_goal "Field goal"). Additionally, tackling below the waist was legalized,[[14]](#cite_note-Camp_and_His_Followers-15) and a static [line of scrimmage](/wiki/Line_of_scrimmage "Line of scrimmage") was instituted.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

Despite these new rules, football remained a violent sport. Dangerous mass-formations like the [flying wedge](/wiki/Flying_wedge "Flying wedge") resulted in serious injuries and deaths.[[18]](#cite_note-19) A 1905 peak of 19 fatalities nationwide resulted in a threat by President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") to abolish the game unless major changes were made.[[19]](#cite_note-20) In response, 62 colleges and universities met in New York City to discuss rule changes on December 28, 1905. These proceedings resulted in the formation of the Intercollegiate Athletic Association of the United States, later renamed the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA).[[20]](#cite_note-NCAA-21)

The legal [forward pass](/wiki/Forward_pass "Forward pass") was introduced in 1906, although its effect was initially minimal due to the restrictions placed on its use. The idea of a 40-yard-wider field was opposed by Harvard due to the size of the new [Harvard Stadium](/wiki/Harvard_Stadium#Influence_on_American_Football "Harvard Stadium").[[21]](#cite_note-22) Other rule changes introduced that year included the reduction of playing time from 70 to 60 minutes and an increase of the distance required for a first down from 5 to 10 yards (4.6 to 9.1 m). To reduce infighting and dirty play between teams, the [neutral zone](/wiki/Neutral_zone_(gridiron_football) "Neutral zone (gridiron football)") was created along the width of the football before the snap.[[22]](#cite_note-Blondy_Wallace_and_the_Biggest_Football_Scandal_Ever:_1906-23) Scoring was also adjusted: points awarded for field goals were reduced to three in 1909[[15]](#cite_note-NFL1869-16) and points for touchdowns were raised to six in 1912.[[23]](#cite_note-NFL1911-24) Also in 1912, the field was shortened to 100 yards (91 m) long, two 10-yard-long (9.1 m) end zones were created, and teams were given four downs instead of three to advance the ball 10 yards (9.1 m).[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-Danzig1956-26) The [roughing the passer](/wiki/Roughing_the_passer "Roughing the passer") penalty was implemented in 1914, and eligible players were first allowed to catch the ball anywhere on the field in 1918.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

### Professional era

On November 12, 1892, [Pudge Heffelfinger](/wiki/Pudge_Heffelfinger "Pudge Heffelfinger") was paid $500 (equivalent to $17,917 in 2025) to play a game for the [Allegheny Athletic Association](/wiki/Allegheny_Athletic_Association "Allegheny Athletic Association") against the [Pittsburgh Athletic Club](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Athletic_Club_(football) "Pittsburgh Athletic Club (football)"). This is the first recorded instance of a player being [paid to participate in a game of American football](/wiki/Professional_football_(gridiron) "Professional football (gridiron)"), although many athletic clubs in the 1880s offered indirect benefits, such as helping players attain employment, giving out trophies or watches that players could pawn for money, or paying double in expense money. Despite these extra benefits, the game had a strict sense of amateurism at the time, and direct payment to players was frowned upon, if not prohibited outright.[[27]](#cite_note-birth-28)

Over time, [professional play](/wiki/Professional_football_(gridiron) "Professional football (gridiron)") became increasingly common, and with it came rising salaries and unpredictable player movement, as well as the illegal payment of college players who were still in school. The [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), a group of professional teams that was originally established in 1920 as the American Professional Football Association, aimed to solve these problems. This new league's stated goals included an end to bidding wars over players, prevention of the use of college players, and abolition of the practice of paying players to leave another team.[[28]](#cite_note-The_First_25_Years-29) By 1922, the NFL had established itself as America's premier professional football league.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

The dominant form of football at the time was played at the [collegiate level](/wiki/College_football "College football"). The upstart NFL received a boost to its legitimacy in 1925, however, when an NFL team, the [Pottsville Maroons](/wiki/Pottsville_Maroons "Pottsville Maroons"), defeated a team of [Notre Dame](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football "Notre Dame Fighting Irish football") all-stars in an [exhibition game](/wiki/1925_NFL_Championship_controversy#Background "1925 NFL Championship controversy").[[30]](#cite_note-The_Curse-31) A greater emphasis on the passing game helped professional football to distinguish itself further from the college game during the late 1930s.[[28]](#cite_note-The_First_25_Years-29) Football, in general, became increasingly popular following the [1958 NFL Championship game](/wiki/1958_NFL_Championship_game "1958 NFL Championship game") between the [Baltimore Colts](/wiki/Baltimore_Colts "Baltimore Colts") and the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants"), still referred to as the "Greatest Game Ever Played". The game, a 23–17 overtime victory by the Colts, was seen by millions of television viewers and had a major influence on the popularity of the sport. By the mid-1960s, football was the most popular sport in the United States.[[31]](#cite_note-Greatest_game_ever_played-32) In 1972, polling from [Gallup, Inc.](/wiki/Gallup,_Inc. "Gallup, Inc.") found that twice as many Americans identified football as their favorite sport (38%) as compared to baseball (19%); the sport has remained the most popular in the United States since.[[32]](#cite_note-Gallup_Vault:_Football's_Rise_as_a_US_Spectator_Sport-33)

In 1960, the [American Football League](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League") (AFL) was created to compete with the NFL. The AFL began in relative obscurity but eventually thrived, with an initial television contract with the [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") television network. The AFL's existence forced the conservative NFL to expand to Dallas and Minnesota in an attempt to destroy the new league. Meanwhile, the AFL introduced many new features to professional football in the United States and actively recruited minority players. The AFL also signed several star college players who had also been drafted by NFL teams. Competition for players heated up in 1965, when the AFL [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets") signed rookie [Joe Namath](/wiki/Joe_Namath "Joe Namath") to a then-record $437,000 contract (equivalent to $4.46 million in 2024). A five-year, $40 million [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") television contract followed, which helped to sustain the young league. The bidding war for players ended in 1966 when NFL owners approached the AFL regarding a merger, and the two leagues [agreed on one](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_merger "AFL–NFL merger") that took full effect in 1970. This agreement provided for a common draft that would take place each year, and it instituted an annual World Championship game to be played between the champions of each league. This championship game, which would come to be known as the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), began play at the end of the 1966 season. Once the merger was completed, it was no longer a championship game between two leagues, instead being competed between the [American Football Conference](/wiki/American_Football_Conference "American Football Conference") (AFC) and the [National Football Conference](/wiki/National_Football_Conference "National Football Conference") (NFC).[[33]](#cite_note-The_Second_25_Years-34)

### Modern era of college football

Prior to the 1956 college football season, NCAA schools were organized into an upper [University Division](/wiki/NCAA_University_Division "NCAA University Division") and lower [College Division](/wiki/NCAA_College_Division "NCAA College Division"). In the summer of [1973](/wiki/1973_NCAA_Division_I_football_season "1973 NCAA Division I football season"), the University Division became [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I"), but by 1976, there was a desire to further separate the major football programs from those that were less financially successful, while still allowing their other sports to compete at the top level.[[34]](#cite_note-maydrop-35) In 1978, the NCAA divided Division I football into two subdivisions: [NCAA Division I-A](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") (known today as the Football Bowl Subdivision, or FBS) and [Division I-AA](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision") (known today as the Football Championship Subdivision, or FCS).[[35]](#cite_note-bswb-36) The FCS adopted a single-elimination playoff structure to determine its champion, while the FBS retained its tradition of postseason [bowl games](/wiki/Bowl_games "Bowl games").[[36]](#cite_note-FCS_Championship:_Everything_you_need_to_know-37)
Each bowl game was associated with a particular conference, and earning a spot in a major bowl game was the reward for winning a conference. This arrangement was beneficial to both conferences and bowl games, but it generally prevented the top-ranked teams from meeting in a true national championship game.[[37]](#cite_note-BCS-38)

Because the NCAA itself [does not officially sanction a Division I-FBS national champion](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS"), FBS national champions prior to 1992 were determined by polls, which frequently led to [controversial or "split" national champions](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS#Split_national_championships "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS").[[38]](#cite_note-How_the_college_football_national_championship_has_changed_through_the_decades,_from_polls_and_bowls_to_the_playoff-39) In 1992, the [Bowl Coalition](/wiki/Bowl_Coalition "Bowl Coalition") was created to determine the national champion. It was replaced in 1995 by the [Bowl Alliance](/wiki/Bowl_Alliance "Bowl Alliance"), which itself gave way to the [Bowl Championship Series](/wiki/Bowl_Championship_Series "Bowl Championship Series") (BCS) in 1997.[[37]](#cite_note-BCS-38) All three of these systems consisted of a single national championship game, as well as a set of other major bowl games.[[38]](#cite_note-How_the_college_football_national_championship_has_changed_through_the_decades,_from_polls_and_bowls_to_the_playoff-39) In 2014, the BCS was replaced by the [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff") (CFP), a single-elimination tournament.[[39]](#cite_note-Presidents_get_playoff_plan_right-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)

In another major change, in 2021, following legal pressure and antitrust lawsuits, the NCAA and its conferences began allowing schools to offer [compensation to student athletes](/wiki/Student_athlete_compensation "Student athlete compensation") for the use of their names, images, and likenesses (NIL).[[41]](#cite_note-42) A legal settlement in *[House v. NCAA](/wiki/House_v._NCAA "House v. NCAA")* allowed schools to directly pay players, ending the traditionally amateur nature of college athletics in the United States.[[42]](#cite_note-Historic_House_v._NCAA_settlement_gets_final_approval,_allowing_schools_to_pay_college_athletes-43)

## Teams and positions

Main article: [American football positions](/wiki/American_football_positions "American football positions")

[![Diagram showing a green background with a white horizontal line dividing it into two-halves, with eleven small blue squares representing defense players in a formation above the line, and eleven small red circles representing offense players in another formation below the line, with two text captions "Defense" and "Offense", the former placed above the line and the latter below the line](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/43BaseDefense.svg/250px-43BaseDefense.svg.png)](/wiki/File:43BaseDefense.svg)

A diagram of a typical pre-snap formation. The offense (red) is lined up in a variation of the [I formation](/wiki/I_formation "I formation"), while the defense (blue) is lined up in the [4–3 defense](/wiki/4%E2%80%933_defense "4–3 defense"). Both formations are legal.

A football game is played between two teams of 11 players each.[[43]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._21-44)[[44]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._15-45)[[45]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._11-46) Playing with more on the field is punishable by a penalty.[[43]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._21-44)[[46]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._107-47)[[47]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._71-72-48)[[nb 2]](#cite_note-50) Teams may substitute [any number of their players](/wiki/Free_substitution "Free substitution") between downs;[[49]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_pp._21-22-51)[[50]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_pp._53-54-52)[[51]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._45-46-53) this "platoon" system has resulted in teams utilizing specialized offensive, defensive and [special teams](/wiki/American_football_positions#Special_teams "American football positions") units.[[52]](#cite_note-The_innovator-54) The number of players allowed on an active roster varies by league; the NFL has a 53-man roster,[[53]](#cite_note-The_Anatomy_of_a_53-Man_Roster_in_the_NFL-55) while NCAA Division I allows teams to have up to 105 players on their roster.[[54]](#cite_note-NCAA_removes_scholarship_limits,_aligns_with_House_settlement_as_roster_sizes_evolve_in_new_college_sports_era-56)

Individual players in a football game must be designated with a [uniform number](/wiki/Uniform_number_(American_football) "Uniform number (American football)") between 0 and 99. NFL teams are required to number their players by a league-approved numbering system, and any exceptions must be approved by the [commissioner](/wiki/Commissioner#Sports "Commissioner").[[43]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._21-44) NCAA and NFHS teams are "strongly advised" to number their offensive players according to a league-suggested numbering scheme.[[55]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_pp._21-22-57)[[56]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._16-17-58)

Women are [eligible to play](/wiki/List_of_female_gridiron_football_players "List of female gridiron football players") in high school, college, and professional football, but most football players are men. No woman has ever played in the NFL, but women have played in high school and college football games.[[57]](#cite_note-For_women,_tackling_NFL_is_a_long_shot-59) In 2018, women and girls comprised 0.5% of players in [Pop Warner Little Scholars](/wiki/Pop_Warner_Little_Scholars "Pop Warner Little Scholars") youth football,[[58]](#cite_note-More_Girls_Are_Playing_Football-60) 0.2% of players in high school football,[[59]](#cite_note-More_High_School_Girls_Are_Playing_Tackle_Football_Than_Ever-61) and 10.9% of Americans who report playing tackle football.[[58]](#cite_note-More_Girls_Are_Playing_Football-60)

### Offensive unit

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Hurricanes_Quarterback.JPG/250px-Hurricanes_Quarterback.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hurricanes_Quarterback.JPG)

A quarterback for the [Kiel Baltic Hurricanes](/wiki/Kiel_Baltic_Hurricanes "Kiel Baltic Hurricanes") in Germany under center and ready to take the snap

The role of the offensive unit is to advance the football down the field with the ultimate goal of scoring a [touchdown](/wiki/Touchdown "Touchdown").[[60]](#cite_note-NFL_in_a_nutshell-62)

The offensive team must line up in a legal [formation](/wiki/Formation_(American_football) "Formation (American football)") before they can snap the ball. An offensive formation is considered illegal if there are more than four players in the [backfield](/wiki/Glossary_of_American_football#backfield "Glossary of American football") or fewer than five players numbered 50–79 on the offensive line.[[44]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._15-45)[[61]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_pp._21-24-63)[[62]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._57-58-64) Players can line up temporarily in a position whose eligibility is different from what their number permits as long as they report the change immediately to the referee, who then informs the defensive team of the change.[[63]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_pp._36,_40-65) Neither team's players, except the [center](/wiki/Center_(gridiron_football) "Center (gridiron football)") (C), are allowed to line up in or cross the neutral zone until the ball is snapped. Interior offensive linemen are not allowed to move until the snap of the ball.[[64]](#cite_note-Common_Penalties_in_American_Football-66)

The main backfield positions are the [quarterback](/wiki/Quarterback "Quarterback") (QB), [running back](/wiki/Running_back "Running back") (RB), and [fullback](/wiki/Fullback_(gridiron_football) "Fullback (gridiron football)") (FB). The quarterback is the leader of the offense. Either the quarterback or a coach calls the plays. Quarterbacks typically inform the rest of the offense of the play in the [huddle](/wiki/Huddle#American_football "Huddle") before the team lines up. The quarterback lines up behind the center to take the snap and then hands the ball off, throws it, or runs with it.[[60]](#cite_note-NFL_in_a_nutshell-62) The primary role of the running back is to carry the ball on running plays. Halfbacks may also serve as receivers. Fullbacks tend to be larger than halfbacks and function primarily as blockers, but they are sometimes used as runners in short-yardage or goal-line situations.[[65]](#cite_note-Football_Players'_Roles_in_Team_Offense_and_Defense-67) They are seldom used as receivers.[[66]](#cite_note-Fullbacks_back_en_vogue-68)

The [offensive line](/wiki/Lineman_(gridiron_football)#offensive_line "Lineman (gridiron football)") (OL) consists of several players whose primary function is to [block](/wiki/Blocking_(American_football) "Blocking (American football)") members of the defensive line from tackling the ball carrier on running plays or [sacking](/wiki/Quarterback_sack "Quarterback sack") the quarterback on passing plays.[[65]](#cite_note-Football_Players'_Roles_in_Team_Offense_and_Defense-67) The most important role on the offensive line is the center, who is not just responsible for blocking and snapping the ball, but also for handling line adjustments and pre-snap strategy.[[67]](#cite_note-Rittenberg:_Meet_the_smartest_guys_on_the_field-69) On either side of the center are the [guards](/wiki/Guard_(gridiron_football) "Guard (gridiron football)") (G), while [tackles](/wiki/Tackle_(gridiron_football_position) "Tackle (gridiron football position)") (T) line up outside the guards.

The principal receivers are the [wide receivers](/wiki/Wide_receiver "Wide receiver") (WR) and the [tight ends](/wiki/Tight_end "Tight end") (TE).[[68]](#cite_note-Football's_Offensive_Team:_The_Receivers-70) Wide receivers line up on or near the line of scrimmage, split outside the line. The main goal of the wide receiver is to catch passes thrown by the quarterback,[[65]](#cite_note-Football_Players'_Roles_in_Team_Offense_and_Defense-67) but they may also function as decoys or as blockers during running plays. Tight ends line up outside the tackles and function both as receivers and as blockers.[[65]](#cite_note-Football_Players'_Roles_in_Team_Offense_and_Defense-67)

### Defensive unit

[![Photograph of defensive players tackling an offensive player who has just lost control of the football](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Arian_Foster_fumble.jpg/250px-Arian_Foster_fumble.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arian_Foster_fumble.jpg)

Defensive players with the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") forcing [Arian Foster](/wiki/Arian_Foster "Arian Foster"), a running back with the [Houston Texans](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans"), to fumble the ball.

The role of the defense is to prevent the offense from scoring by [tackling](/wiki/Tackle_(football_move)#American_and_Canadian_football "Tackle (football move)") the ball carrier or by forcing [turnovers](/wiki/Turnover_(gridiron_football) "Turnover (gridiron football)"). Turnovers include [interceptions](/wiki/Interception "Interception") (a defender catching a forward pass intended for the offense) and forced [fumbles](/wiki/Fumble "Fumble") (taking possession of the ball from the ball-carrier).[[60]](#cite_note-NFL_in_a_nutshell-62)

The [defensive line](/wiki/Defensive_line "Defensive line") (DL) consists of [defensive ends](/wiki/Defensive_end "Defensive end") (DE) and [defensive tackles](/wiki/Defensive_tackle "Defensive tackle") (DT). Defensive ends line up on the ends of the line, while defensive tackles line up inside, between the defensive ends. The primary responsibilities of defensive ends and defensive tackles are to stop running plays on the outside and inside, respectively, to pressure the quarterback on passing plays, and to occupy the line so that the [linebackers](/wiki/Linebacker "Linebacker") can break through.[[65]](#cite_note-Football_Players'_Roles_in_Team_Offense_and_Defense-67)

Linebackers line up behind the defensive line but in front of the defensive backfield. They are divided into two types: middle linebackers (MLB) and outside linebackers (OLB). Linebackers tend to serve as the defensive leaders and call the defensive plays, given their vantage point of the offensive backfield. Their roles include defending the run, pressuring the quarterback, and tackling backs, wide receivers, and tight ends in the passing game.[[69]](#cite_note-Football's_Defensive_Team:_The_Linebackers-71)

The [defensive backfield](/wiki/Defensive_back "Defensive back"), often called the secondary, consists of [cornerbacks](/wiki/Cornerback "Cornerback") (CB) and [safeties](/wiki/Safety_(gridiron_football_position) "Safety (gridiron football position)") (S). Safeties are themselves divided into free safeties (FS) and strong safeties (SS).[[65]](#cite_note-Football_Players'_Roles_in_Team_Offense_and_Defense-67) Cornerbacks line up outside the defensive formation, typically opposite a receiver to be able to cover them. Safeties line up between the cornerbacks but farther back in the secondary. Safeties tend to be viewed as "the last line of defense" and are responsible for stopping deep passing plays as well as breakout running plays.[[65]](#cite_note-Football_Players'_Roles_in_Team_Offense_and_Defense-67)

### Special teams unit

[![A placekicker attempts a field goal by kicking the ball from the hands of a holder.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Connor_Barth_attempts_field_goal_8_November_2015_151108-F-ID984-017.jpg/250px-Connor_Barth_attempts_field_goal_8_November_2015_151108-F-ID984-017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Connor_Barth_attempts_field_goal_8_November_2015_151108-F-ID984-017.jpg)

[Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") placekicker [Connor Barth](/wiki/Connor_Barth "Connor Barth") attempts a field goal by kicking the ball from the hands of a holder. This is the standard method to score field goals or extra points.[[70]](#cite_note-PFRA-72)

The special teams unit is responsible for all kicking plays. The special teams unit of the team in control of the ball tries to execute field goal (FG) attempts, [punts](/wiki/Punt_(gridiron_football) "Punt (gridiron football)"), and [kickoffs](/wiki/Kickoff_(gridiron_football) "Kickoff (gridiron football)"), while the opposing team's unit will aim to block or return them.[[60]](#cite_note-NFL_in_a_nutshell-62)

Three positions are specific to the field goal and PAT (point-after-touchdown) unit: the [placekicker](/wiki/Placekicker "Placekicker") (K or PK), [holder](/wiki/Holder_(American_football) "Holder (American football)") (H), and [long snapper](/wiki/Long_snapper "Long snapper") (LS). The long snapper's job is to snap the football to the holder, who will catch and position it for the placekicker. There is not usually a holder on kickoffs, because the ball is kicked off a tee; however, a holder may be used in certain situations, such as if wind is preventing the ball from remaining upright on the tee. The player on the receiving team who catches the ball is known as the [kickoff returner](/wiki/Kickoff_returner "Kickoff returner") (KR).[[71]](#cite_note-The_Role_of_Special_Teams_in_a_Football_Game-73)

The positions specific to punt plays are the [punter](/wiki/Punter_(football) "Punter (football)") (P), long snapper, [upback](/wiki/Upback "Upback"), and [gunner](/wiki/Gunner_(American_football) "Gunner (American football)"). The long snapper snaps the football directly to the punter, who then drops and kicks it before it hits the ground. Gunners line up split outside the line and race down the field, aiming to tackle the [punt returner](/wiki/Punt_returner "Punt returner") (PR)—the player who catches the punt. Upbacks line up a short distance behind the line of scrimmage, providing additional protection to the punter.[[72]](#cite_note-Football_Special_Teams:_Players_on_a_Punt_Team-74)

## Rules

Main article: [American football rules](/wiki/American_football_rules "American football rules")

### Scoring

[![A player pursued by an opponent dives into the end zone in front of an official to score a touchdown.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/2006_Navy_-_Tusla.jpg/250px-2006_Navy_-_Tusla.jpg)](/wiki/File:2006_Navy_-_Tusla.jpg)

A player for the [Navy Midshipmen](/wiki/Navy_Midshipmen_football "Navy Midshipmen football") (dark jersey) scores a touchdown while a defender from the [Tulsa Golden Hurricane](/wiki/Tulsa_Golden_Hurricane_football "Tulsa Golden Hurricane football") (in white) looks on. The goal line is marked by the small orange pylon.

In football, the winner is the team that has scored more points at the end of the game. There are multiple ways to score in a football game. The [touchdown](/wiki/Touchdown "Touchdown") (TD), worth six points, is the most valuable scoring play. A touchdown is scored when a live ball is advanced into, caught, or recovered in the opposing team's end zone.[[60]](#cite_note-NFL_in_a_nutshell-62) The scoring team then attempts a try, more commonly known as the point(s)-after-touchdown (PAT) or conversion, which is a single scoring opportunity. If the PAT is scored by a place kick or drop kick through the goal posts, it is worth one point, typically called the extra point. If the PAT is scored by what would normally be a touchdown, it is worth two points; this is known as a [two-point conversion](/wiki/Two-point_conversion "Two-point conversion"). In general, the extra point is almost always successful, while the two-point conversion is a much riskier play with a higher probability of failure; accordingly, extra point attempts are far more common than two-point conversion attempts.[[73]](#cite_note-Duke-75)

A [field goal](/wiki/Field_goal "Field goal") (FG), worth three points, is scored when the ball is place kicked or drop kicked through the uprights and over the crossbars of the defense's goalposts.[[74]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._57-59-76)[[75]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._79-80-77)[[76]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._66-78) Almost all field goal attempts are done via place kick. While drop kicks were common in the early days of the sport, the shape of modern footballs makes it difficult to reliably drop kick the ball. The last successful scoring play by drop kick in the NFL was accomplished in 2006; prior to that, the last successful drop kick had been made in 1941.[[77]](#cite_note-79) After a PAT attempt or successful field goal, the scoring team must kick the ball off to the other team.[[78]](#cite_note-Beginner's_Guide_to_Football-80)

A [safety](/wiki/Safety_(gridiron_football_score) "Safety (gridiron football score)") is scored when the ball carrier is tackled in the carrier's own end zone. Safeties are worth two points, which are awarded to the defense.[[60]](#cite_note-NFL_in_a_nutshell-62) In addition, the team that conceded the safety must kick the ball to the scoring team via a [free kick](/wiki/Safety_(gridiron_football_score)#Resuming_play_after_a_safety "Safety (gridiron football score)").[[79]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._60-81) Safeties are the least common method of scoring in football.[[80]](#cite_note-Do_Firms_Maximize-82)

### Field and equipment

Main article: [American football field](/wiki/American_football_field "American football field")

[![Photograph of a football field taken from the end zone showing goal posts in the foreground](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Edward_Jones_Dome_endzone_view.jpg/250px-Edward_Jones_Dome_endzone_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_Jones_Dome_endzone_view.jpg)

The football field at [The Dome at America's Center](/wiki/The_Dome_at_America%27s_Center "The Dome at America's Center") in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis") as seen from behind one end zone. The tall, yellow goal posts mark where the ball must pass for a successful field goal or extra point. The large, rectangular area marked with the team name is the end zone.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Amerikansk_fotboll_i_plast_-_2025.jpg/250px-Amerikansk_fotboll_i_plast_-_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amerikansk_fotboll_i_plast_-_2025.jpg)

An American football.

Football games are played on a rectangular [field](/wiki/American_football_field "American football field") that measures 120 yards (110 m) long and 53+1⁄3 yards (48.8 m) wide. Lines marked along the ends and sides of the field are known as the end lines and [sidelines](/wiki/Sidelines "Sidelines"). [Goal lines](/wiki/Goal_line_(American_football) "Goal line (American football)") are marked 10 yards (9.1 m) inward from each end line.[[81]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._v,_1-83)[[82]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_pp._18-19,_23–24-84)[[83]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._11-12,_13,_28-85)

Weighted pylons are placed along each sideline, on the inside corner of the intersections with the goal lines and end lines. White markings on the field identify the distance from the end zone. Inbound lines, or [hash marks](/wiki/Hash_marks "Hash marks"), are short parallel lines that mark off 1-yard (0.91 m) increments. [Yard lines](/wiki/Yard_lines "Yard lines"), which can run the width of the field, are marked every 5 yards (4.6 m). A one-yard-wide line is placed at each end of the field; this line is marked at the center of the two-yard line in professional play and at the three-yard line in college play. Numerals that display the distance from the closest goal line in yards are placed on both sides of the field every ten yards.[[81]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._v,_1-83)[[82]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_pp._18-19,_23–24-84)[[83]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._11-12,_13,_28-85)

[Goalposts](/wiki/Goalposts "Goalposts") are located at the center of the plane of the two end lines. The crossbar of these posts is 10 feet (3.0 m) above the ground, with vertical uprights at the end of the crossbar 18 feet 6 inches (5.64 m) apart for professional and collegiate play, and 23 feet 4 inches (7.11 m) apart for high school play.[[84]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._2-86)[[85]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._18-87)[[86]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._14-15-88) The uprights extend vertically 35 feet (11 m) on professional fields, a minimum of 10 yards (9.1 m) on college fields, and a minimum of 10 feet (3.0 m) on high school fields. Goal posts are padded at the base, and orange ribbons are normally placed at the tip of each upright as indicators of wind strength and direction.[[84]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._2-86)[[85]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._18-87)[[86]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._14-15-88)

The [football](/wiki/Ball_(gridiron_football) "Ball (gridiron football)") itself is a [prolate spheroid](/wiki/Prolate_spheroid "Prolate spheroid") leather ball, similar to the balls used in rugby or [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football").[[87]](#cite_note-Bounce_of_an_oval_shaped_football-89) At all levels of play, the football is inflated to 12+1⁄2 to 13+1⁄2 psi (86 to 93 kPa), or just under one atmosphere, and weighs 14 to 15 ounces (400 to 430 g);[[86]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._14-15-88)[[88]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._20-90)[[89]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._3-91) beyond that, the exact dimensions vary slightly. In professional play the ball has a long axis of 11 to 11+1⁄4 inches (28 to 29 cm), a long circumference of 28 to 28+1⁄2 inches (71 to 72 cm), and a short circumference of 21 to 21+1⁄4 inches (53 to 54 cm).[[89]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._3-91) In college and high school play the ball has a long axis of 10+7⁄8 to 11+7⁄16 inches (27.6 to 29.1 cm), a long circumference of 27+3⁄4 to 28+1⁄2 inches (70 to 72 cm), and a short circumference of 20+3⁄4 to 21+1⁄4 inches (53 to 54 cm).[[86]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._14-15-88)[[88]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._20-90)

### Duration and time stoppages

Football games last for a total of 60 minutes in professional and college play and are divided into two halves of 30 minutes and four quarters of 15 minutes.[[90]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._14-92)[[91]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._45-93) High school football games are 48 minutes in length with two halves of 24 minutes and four quarters of 12 minutes.[[92]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._38-39-94) The two halves are separated by a [halftime](/wiki/Half-time "Half-time") period, and the first and third quarters are followed by a short break.[[90]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._14-92)[[91]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._45-93)[[93]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._39-95) Before the game starts, the referee and each team's [captain](/wiki/Captain_(sports) "Captain (sports)") meet at midfield for a [coin toss](/wiki/Coin_toss "Coin toss"). The visiting team can call either "heads" or "tails"; the winner of the toss chooses whether to receive or kick off the ball or which goal they wish to defend. They can defer their choice until the second half. Unless the winning team decides to defer, the losing team chooses the option the winning team did not select—to receive, kick, or select a goal to defend to begin the second half. Most teams choose to receive or defer, because choosing to kick the ball to start the game allows the other team to choose which goal to defend.[[94]](#cite_note-TMQ's_all-haiku_NFL_preview-96) Teams switch goals following the first and third quarters.[[95]](#cite_note-How_Football_Game_Time_Is_Measured_in_Quarters-97) If a down is in progress when a quarter ends, play continues until the down is completed.[[96]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._14-18-98)[[97]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_pp._47-53-99)[[98]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._38-45-100) If certain fouls are committed during play while time has expired, the quarter may be extended through an [untimed down](/wiki/Untimed_play "Untimed play").[[99]](#cite_note-Ask_the_official:_Extra-point_ruling_and_untimed_downs-101)

Games last longer than their defined length due to play stoppages—the average NFL game lasts slightly over three hours.[[100]](#cite_note-USA_Today-102) Time in a football game is measured by the game clock. An operator is responsible for starting, stopping and operating the game clock based on the direction of the appropriate [official](/wiki/Official_(American_football) "Official (American football)").[[90]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._14-92)[[101]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_pp._16,_41-103) A separate [play clock](/wiki/Play_clock "Play clock") is used to show the amount of time within which the offense must initiate a play. The play clock is set to 25 seconds after certain administrative stoppages in play and to 40 seconds when play is proceeding without such stoppages. If the offense fails to start a play before the play clock reads "00", a [delay of game](/wiki/Delay_of_game#American "Delay of game") foul is called on the offense.[[96]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._14-18-98)[[102]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_pp._41,_46–47-104)[[103]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2019-105)

### Advancing the ball and downs

[![A quarterback is shown in the process of throwing a forward pass. His offensive linemen are in front of him.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Delhomme_goes_deep.jpg/250px-Delhomme_goes_deep.jpg)](/wiki/File:Delhomme_goes_deep.jpg)

[Carolina Panthers](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers") quarterback [Jake Delhomme](/wiki/Jake_Delhomme "Jake Delhomme") (number 17) in the motion of throwing a forward pass

There are two main ways the offense can advance the ball: [running](/wiki/Rush_(gridiron_football) "Rush (gridiron football)") and [passing](/wiki/Forward_pass#Gridiron_football "Forward pass"). In a typical play, the center passes the ball backwards and between their legs to the quarterback in a process known as the [snap](/wiki/Snap_(gridiron_football) "Snap (gridiron football)"). The quarterback then either hands the ball off to a running back, throws the ball, or runs with it. The play ends when the player with the ball is tackled or goes out-of-bounds or a pass hits the ground without a player having caught it. A forward pass can be legally attempted only if the passer is behind the line of scrimmage; only one forward pass can be attempted per down.[[78]](#cite_note-Beginner's_Guide_to_Football-80) As in rugby, players can also [pass the ball backwards](/wiki/Lateral_pass "Lateral pass") at any point during a play.[[104]](#cite_note-106) In the NFL, a down also ends immediately if the runner's helmet comes off.[[105]](#cite_note-When_a_runner's_helmet_comes_off,_he's_down-107)

The offense is given a series of four plays, known as [downs](/wiki/Down_(gridiron_football) "Down (gridiron football)"). If the offense advances ten or more yards in the four downs, they are awarded a new set of four downs. If they fail to advance ten yards, possession of the football is turned over to the defense. In most situations, if the offense reaches their fourth down they will [punt](/wiki/Punt_(American_football) "Punt (American football)") the ball to the other team, which forces them to begin their drive from farther down the field; if they are in [field goal range](/wiki/Field_goal_range "Field goal range"), they might attempt to score a field goal instead.[[78]](#cite_note-Beginner's_Guide_to_Football-80) A group of officials, the chain crew, keeps track of both the downs and the distance measurements.[[106]](#cite_note-The_Orchestration_of_the_Chain_Gang-108) The spot needed to secure a first down is known as the line to gain.[[107]](#cite_note-Terms_Glossary-109) On television, a yellow line is electronically superimposed on the field to show the line to gain to the viewing audience.[[108]](#cite_note-Yellow_line-110)

### Kicking

There are two categories of kicks in football: scrimmage kicks, which can be executed by the offensive team on any down from behind or on the line of scrimmage,[[109]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._50-111)[[110]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._34-112)[[111]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._32-113) and free kicks.[[112]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._6-114)[[113]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._30-115)[[114]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._27-116) The free kicks are the [kickoff](/wiki/Kickoff_(American_football) "Kickoff (American football)"), which starts the first and third quarters and overtime and follows a try attempt or a successful field goal, and the safety kick, which follows a safety.[[110]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._34-112)[[115]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_pp._8-9-117)[[116]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._31-32-118)

On a kickoff, the ball is placed at the 35-yard line of the kicking team in professional and college play and at the 40-yard line in high school play. The ball may be drop kicked or place kicked. If a place kick is chosen, the ball can be placed on the ground or a tee; a holder may be used in either case. On a safety kick, the kicking team kicks the ball from their own 20-yard line. They can punt, drop kick or place kick the ball, but a tee may not be used in professional play. Any member of the receiving team may catch or advance the ball. The ball may be recovered by the kicking team once it has gone at least ten yards and has touched the ground or has been touched by any member of the receiving team.[[117]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_pp._29-30-119)[[118]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_pp._61-64-120)[[119]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._15,_46,_52–53-121)

The three types of scrimmage kicks are place kicks, drop kicks, and punts. Only place kicks and drop kicks can score points.[[74]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._57-59-76)[[75]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_p._79-80-77)[[76]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._66-78) The place kick is the standard method used to score points,[[70]](#cite_note-PFRA-72) because the pointy shape of the football makes it difficult to reliably drop kick.[[70]](#cite_note-PFRA-72) Once the ball has been kicked from a scrimmage kick, it can be advanced by the kicking team only if it is caught or recovered behind the line of scrimmage. If it is touched or recovered by the kicking team beyond this line, it becomes dead at the spot where it was touched.[[120]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_pp._33-34,_50–53-122)[[121]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_pp._55-56,_63–64-123)[[122]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._49,_53–54-124) The kicking team is prohibited from interfering with the receiver's opportunity to catch the ball. The receiving team has the option of signaling for a [fair catch](/wiki/Fair_catch "Fair catch"), which prohibits the defense from blocking into or tackling the receiver. The play ends as soon as the ball is caught, and the ball may not be advanced.[[123]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_p._7-125)[[124]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011–2012,_pp._30,_66–67-126)[[125]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_p._2756,-127)

### Officials and fouls

See also: [Official (American football)](/wiki/Official_(American_football) "Official (American football)"), [Chain crew](/wiki/Chain_crew "Chain crew"), and [Penalty (gridiron football)](/wiki/Penalty_(gridiron_football) "Penalty (gridiron football)")

[![Seven officials are pictured meeting at the infield. Officials meeting at midfield](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/American_football_referees.jpg/250px-American_football_referees.jpg)](/wiki/File:American_football_referees.jpg)

Officials meeting at midfield

[![Officials are using a chain to measure for a first down.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/First_down_measurement_at_EWU_at_Cal_2009-09-12.JPG/250px-First_down_measurement_at_EWU_at_Cal_2009-09-12.JPG)](/wiki/File:First_down_measurement_at_EWU_at_Cal_2009-09-12.JPG)

Officials in a game between [Eastern Washington](/wiki/Eastern_Washington_Eagles_football "Eastern Washington Eagles football") and [Cal Berkeley](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears_football "California Golden Bears football") use the chains to measure for a first down. Here, the ball is just short of the pole and therefore short of a first down.

[![Photograph of a down indicator box on a pole](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/First_down_marker.png/250px-First_down_marker.png)](/wiki/File:First_down_marker.png)

A modern down indicator box is mounted on a pole and is used to mark the current line of scrimmage. The number on the marker is changed using a dial.

Officials are responsible for enforcing game rules and monitoring the clock. All officials carry a [whistle](/wiki/Whistle "Whistle") and wear black-and-white striped shirts and black hats, except for the referee, whose hat is white. Each carries a [weighted yellow flag](/wiki/Penalty_flag "Penalty flag") that is thrown to the ground to signal that a [foul](/wiki/Penalty_(gridiron_football) "Penalty (gridiron football)") has been called. An official who spots multiple fouls will throw their hat as a secondary signal.[[126]](#cite_note-American_Football_Officials-128) Women can serve as officials; [Sarah Thomas](/wiki/Sarah_Thomas_(American_football_official) "Sarah Thomas (American football official)") became the NFL's first female official in 2015.[[127]](#cite_note-129) The seven officials (of a standard seven-man crew; lower levels of play up to the college level use fewer officials) on the field are each tasked with a different set of responsibilities:[[126]](#cite_note-American_Football_Officials-128)

* The referee: is positioned behind and to the side of the offensive backs. The referee is charged with oversight and control of the game and is the authority on the score, the down number, and any rule interpretations in discussions among the other officials. The referee announces all penalties and discusses the infraction with the offending team's captain, monitors for illegal hits against the quarterback, makes requests for first-down measurements, and notifies the head coach whenever a player is ejected. The referee positions themselves to the passing arm side of the quarterback. In most games, the referee is responsible for spotting the football prior to a play from scrimmage.
* The umpire: is positioned in the defensive backfield, except in the NFL, where the umpire is positioned lateral to the referee on the opposite side of the formation. The umpire watches play along the line of scrimmage to make sure that no more than 11 offensive players are on the field before the snap, and that no offensive linemen are [illegally downfield](/wiki/Ineligible_receiver_downfield "Ineligible receiver downfield") on pass plays. The umpire monitors contact between offensive and defensive linemen and calls most of the [holding](/wiki/Holding_(American_football) "Holding (American football)") penalties. The umpire records the number of timeouts taken and the winner of the coin toss and game score. They also assist the referee in situations involving possession of the ball close to the line of scrimmage, determine whether player equipment is legal, and dry wet balls prior to the snap if a game is played in rain.
* The back judge: is positioned deep in the defensive backfield, behind the umpire. The back judge ensures that the defensive team has no more than 11 players on the field and determines whether catches are legal, whether field goal or extra point attempts are good, and whether a [pass interference](/wiki/Pass_interference "Pass interference") violation occurred. The back judge is also responsible for the play clock, the time between each play, when a visible play clock is not used.
* The head linesman/down judge: is positioned on one end of the line of scrimmage. The head linesman/down judge watches for any line-of-scrimmage and illegal use-of-hands violations and assists the line judge with illegal shift or illegal motion calls. The head linesman/down judge also rules on out-of-bounds calls that happen on their side of the field, oversees the chain crew, and marks the forward progress of a runner when a play has been whistled dead.
* The side judge: is positioned twenty yards downfield of the head linesman. The side judge mainly duplicates the functions of the field judge. On field goal and extra point attempts, the side judge is positioned lateral to the umpire.
* The line judge: is positioned on the end of the line of scrimmage, opposite the head linesman. They supervise player substitutions, the line of scrimmage during punts, and game timing. The line judge notifies the referee when time has expired at the end of a quarter and notifies the head coach of the home team when five minutes remain for halftime. In the NFL, the line judge also alerts the referee when [two minutes remain in the half](/wiki/Two-minute_warning "Two-minute warning"). If the clock malfunctions or becomes inoperable, the line judge becomes the official timekeeper.
* The field judge: is positioned twenty yards downfield from the line judge. The field judge monitors and controls the [play clock](/wiki/Play_clock "Play clock"), counts the number of defensive players on the field, and watches for offensive pass interference and illegal use-of-hands violations by offensive players. The field judge also makes decisions regarding catches, recoveries, the ball spot when a player goes out of bounds, and illegal touching of fumbled balls that have crossed the line of scrimmage. On field goal and extra point attempts, the field judge is stationed under the upright opposite the back judge.
* The center judge: is an eighth official used only in the top level of college football. The center judge stands lateral to the referee, the same way the umpire does in the NFL. The center judge is responsible for spotting the football after each play and has many of the same responsibilities as the referee, except announcing penalties.

Another set of officials, the [chain crew](/wiki/Chain_crew "Chain crew"), are responsible for moving the chains. The chains, consisting of two large sticks with a 10-yard-long chain between them, are used to measure for a first down. The chain crew stays on the sidelines during the game, but if requested by the officials, they will briefly bring the chains onto the field to measure. A typical chain crew will have at least three people—two members of the chain crew will hold either of the two sticks, while a third will hold the down marker. The down marker, a large stick with a dial on it, is flipped after each play to indicate the current down and is typically moved to the approximate spot of the ball. The chain crew system has been used for over 100 years and is considered an accurate measure of distance, rarely subject to criticism from either side.[[106]](#cite_note-The_Orchestration_of_the_Chain_Gang-108)

## Safety and brain health

See also: [Protective equipment in gridiron football](/wiki/Protective_equipment_in_gridiron_football "Protective equipment in gridiron football") and [Health issues in American football](/wiki/Health_issues_in_American_football "Health issues in American football")

[![Photograph of a player wearing a helmet, with shoulder pads and thigh pads visible under their uniform](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Vince_Agnew.jpg/250px-Vince_Agnew.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vince_Agnew.jpg)

[Vince Agnew](/wiki/Vince_Agnew "Vince Agnew"), then with the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins"), wearing a helmet with his shoulder pads and thigh pads visible under his uniform

Football is a full-contact sport, and injuries are relatively common. Most injuries occur during training sessions, particularly ones that involve contact between players.[[128]](#cite_note-Sports_Medicine-130) To try to prevent injuries, players are required to wear a set of equipment. At a minimum players must wear a [football helmet](/wiki/Football_helmet "Football helmet") and a set of [shoulder pads](/wiki/Shoulder_pads_(sport) "Shoulder pads (sport)"), but individual leagues may require additional padding such as thigh pads and guards, knee pads, chest protectors, and [mouthguards](/wiki/Mouthguard "Mouthguard").[[129]](#cite_note-NFL_Rules_2012,_pp._24-27-131)[[130]](#cite_note-NCAA_Rules_2011-2012,_p._22-132)[[131]](#cite_note-NFHS_Rules_2012,_pp._17-19-133) Most injuries occur in the lower extremities, particularly in the knee, but a significant number also affect the upper extremities. The most common types of injuries are [strains](/wiki/Strain_(injury) "Strain (injury)"), [sprains](/wiki/Sprain "Sprain"), [bruises](/wiki/Bruise "Bruise"), [fractures](/wiki/Bone_fracture "Bone fracture"), [dislocations](/wiki/Joint_dislocation "Joint dislocation"), and [concussions](/wiki/Concussion "Concussion").[[128]](#cite_note-Sports_Medicine-130)

Repeated [concussions](/wiki/Concussions_in_American_football "Concussions in American football") (and possibly sub-concussive head impacts[[132]](#cite_note-134)) can increase a person's risk in later life for CTE ([chronic traumatic encephalopathy](/wiki/Chronic_traumatic_encephalopathy "Chronic traumatic encephalopathy")) and health issues such as [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia"), [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), and [depression](/wiki/Mood_disorder "Mood disorder").[[133]](#cite_note-135) Concussions are often caused by helmet-to-helmet or upper-body contact between opposing players, although helmets have prevented more serious injuries such as [skull fractures](/wiki/Skull_fracture "Skull fracture").[[134]](#cite_note-Can_Football_Finally_Tackle_Its_Injury_Problem?-136) Various programs are aiming to reduce concussions by reducing the frequency of helmet-to-helmet hits; [USA Football](/wiki/USA_Football "USA Football")'s "Heads Up Football" program aims to reduce concussions in youth football by teaching coaches and players about the signs of a concussion, the proper way to wear football equipment and ensure it fits, and proper tackling methods that avoid helmet-to-helmet contact.[[135]](#cite_note-Heads_Up_Football-137) However, a study in the *Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine* found that Heads Up Football was ineffective; the same study noted that more extensive reforms implemented by [Pop Warner Little Scholars](/wiki/Pop_Warner_Little_Scholars "Pop Warner Little Scholars") and its member teams were effective in significantly reducing concussion rates.[[136]](#cite_note-138)

A 2018 study performed by the VA Boston Healthcare System and the [Boston University School of Medicine](/wiki/Boston_University_School_of_Medicine "Boston University School of Medicine") found that tackle football before age 12 was correlated with earlier onset of symptoms of CTE, but not with increased symptom severity. More specifically, each year a player played tackle football under age 12 predicted earlier onset of cognitive, behavioral, and mood problems by an average of two and a half years.[[137]](#cite_note-Study_finds_youth_football_tied_to_earlier_symptoms_of_CTE,_ESPN,_April_30,_2018-139)[[138]](#cite_note-Austin_American-Statesman,_May_25,_2018-140)[[139]](#cite_note-Annals_of_Neurology,_30_April_2018-141)

To help mitigate injuries, the NCAA recommends limiting full contact (tackling to the ground) practices, avoiding full contact on consecutive days, and no more than 20 hours of practice per week. Players must receive one day off per week.[[140]](#cite_note-Spring_football_practice_recommendations_advance-142)

## Leagues and tournaments

See also: [Comparisons between the National Football League and NCAA football](/wiki/Comparisons_between_the_National_Football_League_and_NCAA_football "Comparisons between the National Football League and NCAA football")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/High_School_Football_Game_at_New_Canaan_High_School%2C_CT.jpg/250px-High_School_Football_Game_at_New_Canaan_High_School%2C_CT.jpg)](/wiki/File:High_School_Football_Game_at_New_Canaan_High_School,_CT.jpg)

A [high school football](/wiki/High_school_football "High school football") game at [New Canaan High School](/wiki/New_Canaan_High_School "New Canaan High School") in [New Canaan, Connecticut](/wiki/New_Canaan,_Connecticut "New Canaan, Connecticut")

The [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) are the most popular football leagues in the United States.[[141]](#cite_note-Oregon_Live-143) The National Football League was founded in 1920[[142]](#cite_note-NFL_founded_in_Canton-144) and has since become the largest and most popular sport in the United States.[[143]](#cite_note-China_fast_catching_American_football_fever-145) The NFL has the highest average attendance of any sporting league in the world, with an average attendance of 66,960 during the [2011 NFL season](/wiki/2011_NFL_season "2011 NFL season").[[144]](#cite_note-And_the_silver_goes_to...-146) The NFL championship game is called the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), and is among the biggest events in club sports worldwide.[[145]](#cite_note-Elite_clubs_on_Uefa_gravy_train_as_Super_Bowl_knocked_off_perch-147) It is played between the champions of the [National Football Conference](/wiki/National_Football_Conference "National Football Conference") (NFC) and the [American Football Conference](/wiki/American_Football_Conference "American Football Conference") (AFC), and its winner is awarded the [Vince Lombardi Trophy](/wiki/Vince_Lombardi_Trophy "Vince Lombardi Trophy").[[146]](#cite_note-Let's_Learn_About:_The_Vince_Lombardi_Trophy-148)

[College football](/wiki/College_football "College football") is the third-most popular sport in the United States, behind professional football and professional baseball, respectively.[[147]](#cite_note-Harris_Poll_2012-149) The NCAA, the largest collegiate organization, is divided into three Divisions: [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I"), [Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II") and [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III").[[148]](#cite_note-About_the_NCAA-150) Division I is the highest level of play, while Division III is the lowest; Division I and II programs can offer athletic scholarships to student-athletes, while Division III programs cannot.[[149]](#cite_note-Playing_a_Sport_in_Each_NCAA_Division:_What_to_Know-151) Division I football is further divided into two subdivisions: the [Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/Football_Bowl_Subdivision "Football Bowl Subdivision") (FBS) and the [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/Football_Championship_Subdivision "Football Championship Subdivision") (FCS).[[35]](#cite_note-bswb-36) The champions of each level of play are determined through playoff systems.[[150]](#cite_note-Postseason_Football-152) Other national college football organizations include the [National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics](/wiki/National_Association_of_Intercollegiate_Athletics "National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics") (NAIA), which comprises around 90 smaller colleges, and the [National Junior College Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Junior_College_Athletic_Association "National Junior College Athletic Association") (NJCAA), whose membership consists of [two-year colleges](/wiki/Junior_college "Junior college").[[151]](#cite_note-How_do_college_football_conferences_work?-153)

[High school football](/wiki/High_school_football "High school football") is the most popular sport in the United States played by boys. The NFHS is the largest organization for high school football, with member associations in all [50 states](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States "List of states and territories of the United States") as well as the [District of Columbia](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."). USA Football is the governing body for youth and amateur football,[[152]](#cite_note-NFHS_and_USA_Football_Create_Football_Coaching_Course-154) and Pop Warner Little Scholars is the largest organization for youth football.[[153]](#cite_note-Trying_to_Reduce_Head_Injuries,_Youth_Football_Limits_Practices-155)

### Other professional leagues

#### Rival leagues

The most successful league to directly compete with the NFL was the American Football League (AFL), which existed from 1960 to 1969. The AFL became a significant rival in [1964](/wiki/1964_American_Football_League_season "1964 American Football League season") before signing a five-year, US$36 million television deal with NBC. AFL teams began signing NFL players to contracts, and the league's popularity grew to challenge that of the NFL.
