The two leagues merged in the [1970 season](/wiki/1970_NFL_season "1970 NFL season"), and all the AFL teams joined the NFL. An earlier league, the [All-America Football Conference](/wiki/All-America_Football_Conference "All-America Football Conference") (AAFC), was in play from 1946 to 1949. After it had dissolved, two AAFC teams, the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") and the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers"), became members of the NFL; another member, the [Baltimore Colts](/wiki/Baltimore_Colts_(1947%E2%80%9350) "Baltimore Colts (1947–50)") joined the league, but folded after just a year in the NFL.[[154]](#cite_note-Off-the-field_competition_yields_game-changing_merger-156)

Other attempts to start rival leagues since the AFL merged with the NFL in 1970 have been far less successful, as professional football salaries and the [NFL's television contracts](/wiki/NFL_on_television "NFL on television") began to escalate out of the reach of competitors and the NFL covered more of the larger cities. The [World Football League](/wiki/World_Football_League "World Football League") (WFL) played for two seasons, in 1974 and 1975, but faced such severe monetary issues it could not pay its players. In its second and final season the WFL attempted to establish a stable credit rating, but the league disbanded before the season could be completed.[[155]](#cite_note-The_Day_The_Money_Ran_Out-157) The [United States Football League](/wiki/United_States_Football_League "United States Football League") (USFL) operated for three seasons from 1983 to 1985. Originally not intended as a rival league, the entry of owners who sought marquee talent and to challenge the NFL led to an escalation in salaries and ensuing financial losses. A subsequent US$1.5 billion antitrust lawsuit against the NFL was successful in court, but the league was awarded only $1 in damages, which was automatically tripled to $3 under antitrust law.[[156]](#cite_note-Twenty_years_later,_USFL_still_brings_fond_memories-158)

#### Complementary national leagues

The original [XFL](/wiki/XFL_(2001) "XFL (2001)"), created in 2001 by [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE") co-founder [Vince McMahon](/wiki/Vince_McMahon "Vince McMahon"), lasted for only one season. Despite television contracts with NBC and [UPN](/wiki/UPN "UPN"), and high expectations, the XFL suffered from low quality of play and poor reception for its use of [tawdry professional wrestling gimmicks](/wiki/Attitude_Era "Attitude Era"), which caused initially high ratings and attendance to collapse.[[157]](#cite_note-No_More_Springtimes_for_the_XFL_as_League_Folds-159) The [United Football League](/wiki/United_Football_League_(2009%E2%80%932012) "United Football League (2009–2012)") (UFL) began in [2009](/wiki/2009_UFL_season "2009 UFL season") but folded after suspending its [2012 season](/wiki/2012_UFL_season "2012 UFL season") after having lost $120 million over just three seasons.[[158]](#cite_note-A_comeback_for_XFL,_but_can_it_win?-160) Another spring league, the [Alliance of American Football](/wiki/Alliance_of_American_Football "Alliance of American Football") lasted [less than one season](/wiki/2019_AAF_season "2019 AAF season"), unable to keep investors.[[159]](#cite_note-disappointed-161)

The XFL was rebooted in [2020](/wiki/XFL_(2020) "XFL (2020)");[[160]](#cite_note-162) after only five weeks of play, the league's operations slowly came to a close due to the ongoing [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States"),[[161]](#cite_note-suspendsoperations-163) The league filed for bankruptcy on April 13[[162]](#cite_note-seekingbuyer-164) and was purchased by actor and professional wrestler [Dwayne "The Rock" Johnson](/wiki/Dwayne_%22The_Rock%22_Johnson "Dwayne \"The Rock\" Johnson"), along with longtime business partner and ex-wife [Dany Garcia](/wiki/Dany_Garcia "Dany Garcia").[[163]](#cite_note-newowners-165) A revived [United States Football League](/wiki/United_States_Football_League_(2022%E2%80%932023) "United States Football League (2022–2023)") began play in 2022[[164]](#cite_note-USFL_back_for_second_season,_says_Fox's_Shanks-166) and quickly faced immediate competition from the XFL, which resumed play in 2023.[[165]](#cite_note-axios-167)[[166]](#cite_note-168) The two leagues merged following the 2023 season, forming a new [United Football League](/wiki/United_Football_League_(2024) "United Football League (2024)") (UFL).[[167]](#cite_note-mergerannounced-169)

### International play

[![Photograph of a Mexican youth division football team](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/03042012Ccm_gamosuma_juvenilA366.JPG/250px-03042012Ccm_gamosuma_juvenilA366.JPG)](/wiki/File:03042012Ccm_gamosuma_juvenilA366.JPG)

Players with one of the youth divisions of the [Borregos Salvajes](/wiki/Borregos_Salvajes "Borregos Salvajes") football program of the [Monterrey Institute of Technology and Higher Education](/wiki/Monterrey_Institute_of_Technology_and_Higher_Education "Monterrey Institute of Technology and Higher Education") in Mexico City

American football leagues exist throughout the world, but the game has yet to achieve the international success and popularity of [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") and [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball").[[168]](#cite_note-170) At the international level, Canada, Mexico, and Japan are considered to be second-tier, while Austria, Germany, and France would rank among a third tier. These countries rank far below the United States, which is dominant at the international level.[[169]](#cite_note-Football_in_Olympics_is_a_dream_that_could_become_a_reality-171)

[NFL Europa](/wiki/NFL_Europe "NFL Europe"), the [developmental league](/wiki/Minor_league "Minor league") of the NFL, operated from 1991 to 1992 and then from 1995 to 2007. At the time of its closure, NFL Europa had five teams based in Germany and one in the Netherlands.[[170]](#cite_note-NFL_Europa_to_cease_operations-172) In Germany, the [German Football League](/wiki/German_Football_League "German Football League") (GFL) has 16 teams and has operated for over 40 seasons, with the league's championship game, the [German Bowl](/wiki/German_Bowl "German Bowl"), closing out each season. The league operates in a [promotion and relegation](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") structure with [German Football League 2](/wiki/German_Football_League_2 "German Football League 2") (GFL2), which also has 16 teams.[[171]](#cite_note-Vicksburg-173) The [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") also operated several teams within NFL Europe during the League's tenure.[[172]](#cite_note-174) The resulting rise in popularity of the sport brought the NFL back to the country in 2007 where they now hold the [NFL International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), currently consisting of four regular season games.[[173]](#cite_note-175)[[174]](#cite_note-176) The continuing interest and growth in both the sport and the series has led to the possible formation of a [potential NFL franchise in London](/wiki/Potential_London_NFL_franchise "Potential London NFL franchise").[[175]](#cite_note-177)[[176]](#cite_note-178) NFL commissioner [Roger Goodell](/wiki/Roger_Goodell "Roger Goodell") aims to host 16 international games each year, enough for each NFL team to play one international game each year.[[177]](#cite_note-Roger_Goodell_Lays_Out_Ambitious_Plan_to_Continue_NFL's_International_Expansion-179)

In the United Kingdom, the [British American Football Association National Leagues](/wiki/BAFA_National_Leagues "BAFA National Leagues") (BAFANL), an amateur competition, is the top level of American football.[[178]](#cite_note-Love_of_game_drives_GB_American_football_hopefuls-180) Teams compete across multiple levels in a [promotion and relegation](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") structure; the league's championship is the [Britbowl](/wiki/Britbowl "Britbowl").[[179]](#cite_note-'The_only_team_that_can_beat_us_is_ourselves':_How_London_Warriors_built_a_dynasty-181) University competition is held through the [British Universities American Football League](/wiki/British_Universities_American_Football_League "British Universities American Football League") (BUAFL), which was formed in 2007.[[180]](#cite_note-VICE_BUAFL-182) In 2008, the league launched a partnership with the NFL, through its partner organization NFL UK.[[181]](#cite_note-183) From 2007 to 2014, the BUAFL grew from 42 teams and 2,460 participants to 75 teams and over 4,100 people involved.[[182]](#cite_note-184)

#### International governance and Olympics

The standard form of American football is not an [Olympic sport](/wiki/Olympic_sport "Olympic sport"), but it was a [demonstration sport](/wiki/Demonstration_sport "Demonstration sport") at the [1932 Summer Olympics](/wiki/American_football_at_the_1932_Summer_Olympics "American football at the 1932 Summer Olympics").[[183]](#cite_note-Football_remains_an_Olympic_long_shot-185) The [International Federation of American Football](/wiki/International_Federation_of_American_Football "International Federation of American Football") (IFAF), an international governing body composed of continental federations, received provisional recognition from the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) in 2014[[184]](#cite_note-Football_takes_step_toward_Olympics,_could_be_medal_sport_in_2024-186) and was fully recognized by the IOC in 2015.[[185]](#cite_note-IOC_grants_full_recognition_to_the_International_Federation_of_American_Football-187) American football federations are present in [Africa](/wiki/IFAF_Africa "IFAF Africa"), [the Americas](/wiki/IFAF_Americas "IFAF Americas"), [Asia](/wiki/IFAF_Asia "IFAF Asia"), [Europe](/wiki/IFAF_Europe "IFAF Europe"), and [Oceania](/wiki/IFAF_Oceania "IFAF Oceania"); a total of 75 national football federations exist as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_football&action=edit).[[186]](#cite_note-188) The IFAF runs tournaments such as the [IFAF World Championship](/wiki/IFAF_World_Championship "IFAF World Championship"), the [IFAF Women's World Championship](/wiki/IFAF_Women%27s_World_Championship "IFAF Women's World Championship"), the [IFAF Junior World Championship](/wiki/IFAF_Junior_World_Championship "IFAF Junior World Championship"), and the [Flag Football World Championship](/wiki/IFAF_Flag_Football_World_Championship "IFAF Flag Football World Championship").[[187]](#cite_note-Championship_Competitions-189)

Obstacles towards the goal of becoming an Olympic sport included the predominant participation of men in international play and the short three-week Olympic schedule. Large team sizes were an additional difficulty, due to the Olympics' set limit of 10,500 athletes and coaches. American football also faced an issue with a lack of global visibility. [Nigel Melville](/wiki/Nigel_Melville "Nigel Melville"), the CEO of [USA Rugby](/wiki/USA_Rugby "USA Rugby"), noted that "American football is recognized globally as a sport, but it's not played globally." To solve these concerns, major effort was put into promoting [flag football](/wiki/Flag_football "Flag football"), a modified version of American football, at the international level.[[169]](#cite_note-Football_in_Olympics_is_a_dream_that_could_become_a_reality-171) Flag football for men and women will [debut as an Olympic sport](/wiki/Flag_football_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Flag football at the Summer Olympics") at the [2028 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2028_Summer_Olympics "2028 Summer Olympics").[[188]](#cite_note-Olympic_hopefuls_and_celebrities_team_up_to_showcase_flag_football:_‘The_sky’s_the_limit’-190)

## Popularity and cultural influence

### United States

Main article: [American football in the United States](/wiki/American_football_in_the_United_States "American football in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Alberstson%27s_Stadium_Record_Attendance_for_Boise_State_Football_in_October_2019.jpg/250px-Alberstson%27s_Stadium_Record_Attendance_for_Boise_State_Football_in_October_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alberstson%27s_Stadium_Record_Attendance_for_Boise_State_Football_in_October_2019.jpg)

The [Boise State Broncos](/wiki/Boise_State_Broncos "Boise State Broncos"), the first football team to design a blue field

"Baseball is still called the national pastime, but football is by far the more popular sport in American society", according to ESPN.com's Sean McAdam.[[189]](#cite_note-Football_leaving_baseball_in_the_dust-191) In a 2014 poll conducted by [Harris Interactive](/wiki/Harris_Insights_%26_Analytics "Harris Insights & Analytics"), professional football ranked as the most popular sport, and college football ranked third behind only professional football and baseball; 46% of participants ranked some form of the game as their favorite sport. Professional football has ranked as the most popular sport in the poll since 1985, when it surpassed baseball for the first time.[[190]](#cite_note-NFL_most_popular_for_30th_year_in_row-192) Professional football is most popular among those who live in the eastern United States and rural areas, while college football is most popular in the southern United States and among people with graduate and post-graduate degrees.[[191]](#cite_note-As_American_as_Mom,_Apple_Pie_and_Football?-193) Football is also the most-played sport by high school and college athletes in the United States. A 2022 survey from [National Football Foundation](/wiki/National_Football_Foundation "National Football Foundation") found that one million high-school athletes played the sport, with another 81,000 college athletes across both the NCAA and the NAIA.[[192]](#cite_note-National_Football_Foundation2023-194)

The Super Bowl is the most popular single-day sporting event in the United States,[[33]](#cite_note-The_Second_25_Years-34) and is among the biggest club sporting events in the world in terms of TV viewership.[[145]](#cite_note-Elite_clubs_on_Uefa_gravy_train_as_Super_Bowl_knocked_off_perch-147) The NFL made approximately $12 billion in revenue in 2022.[[193]](#cite_note-195) Super Bowl games account for eight of the top ten [most-watched broadcasts in American history](/wiki/List_of_most_watched_television_broadcasts_in_the_United_States "List of most watched television broadcasts in the United States"); [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII"), played on February 12, 2023, was watched by a record 115.1 million Americans,[[194]](#cite_note-Super_Bowl_XLIX-196) and is second only to the [Apollo 11](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11") [moon landing](/wiki/Moon_landing "Moon landing") (125 million viewers).[[195]](#cite_note-197) The NFL is the most valuable sports league in the world, with the average team valued at over $8 billion.[[196]](#cite_note-Cowboys_now_worth_a_record_$12.5B_—_10_other_NFL_teams_valued_at_more_than_$8B-198)

American football also plays a significant role in American culture. The day on which the Super Bowl is held is considered [a *de facto* national holiday](/wiki/Super_Bowl_Sunday "Super Bowl Sunday"),[[197]](#cite_note-NFL_has_made_Super_Bowl_Sunday_into_a_holiday,_is_a_three-day_weekend_the_next_step?-199) and in parts of the country like [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), the sport has been compared to a religion.[[198]](#cite_note-In_Texas,_High_School_Football_Is_King-200)[[199]](#cite_note-A_$60_Million_Palace_for_Texas_High_School_Football-201) Football is also linked to other holidays; New Year's Day is traditionally the date for several college football bowl games, including the [Rose Bowl](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_Game "Rose Bowl Game"). However, if New Year's Day is on a Sunday, the bowl games are moved to another date so as not to conflict with the typical NFL Sunday schedule.[[200]](#cite_note-No_New_Year's_Day_Bowl_Games?_Bah,_Humbug-202) [Thanksgiving](/wiki/Thanksgiving_(United_States) "Thanksgiving (United States)") [football](/wiki/American_football_on_Thanksgiving "American football on Thanksgiving") is another American tradition,[[201]](#cite_note-From_Macy's_to_NFL,_Thanksgiving_traditions_explained-203) hosting many high school, college, and professional games.[[202]](#cite_note-Thanksgiving_and_Football:_A_Unique_American_Tradition-204) Implicit rules such as playing through pain and sacrificing for the better of the team are promoted in football culture.[[203]](#cite_note-205)

### Other countries

See also: [American football in Australia](/wiki/American_football_in_Australia "American football in Australia"), [American football in Brazil](/wiki/American_football_in_Brazil "American football in Brazil"), [American football in Japan](/wiki/American_football_in_Japan "American football in Japan"), and [American football in the United Kingdom](/wiki/American_football_in_the_United_Kingdom "American football in the United Kingdom")

[![Photograph of London's Wembley Stadium during the opening ceremony of the 2010 NFL International series showing the field and the stands filled with fans](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/NFL_International_Series_2010.jpg/250px-NFL_International_Series_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:NFL_International_Series_2010.jpg)

Opening ceremony of the 2010 [NFL International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series") at London's [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Suomi-ruotsi_jenkkifutis.jpg/250px-Suomi-ruotsi_jenkkifutis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suomi-ruotsi_jenkkifutis.jpg)

A 2012 football game between national teams of [Finland](/wiki/Finland_national_American_football_team "Finland national American football team") and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_national_American_football_team "Sweden national American football team") at [Sonera Stadium](/wiki/Sonera_Stadium "Sonera Stadium") (now Bolt Arena) in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki")

In [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), the game has a significant following. According to a 2013 poll, 21% of respondents said they followed the NFL "very closely" or "fairly closely", making it the third-most followed league behind the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) and [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") (CFL).[[204]](#cite_note-Super_Bowl_2014:_Comparing_the_NFL's_popularity_in_Canada_and_the_U.S.-206) American football also has a long history in Mexico, which was introduced to the sport in 1896. It was the second-most popular sport in Mexico in the 1950s, with the game being particularly popular in colleges.[[205]](#cite_note-Mexico's_long_love_affair_with_football,_American-style-207) The *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* notes the NFL claims over 16 million fans in Mexico, which places the country third behind the U.S. and Canada.[[206]](#cite_note-NFL's_popularity_in_Mexico_continues_to_grow-208) American football is played in Mexico both professionally and as part of the college sports system.[[207]](#cite_note-American_football_touches_down_in_Germany-209) A professional league, the [Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional](/wiki/Liga_de_F%C3%BAtbol_Americano_Profesional "Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional") (LFA), was founded in 2016.[[208]](#cite_note-210)

[Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") was introduced to the sport in 1934 by [Paul Rusch](/wiki/Paul_Rusch "Paul Rusch"), a [teacher](/wiki/Teacher "Teacher") and [Christian missionary](/wiki/Christian_mission "Christian mission") who helped to establish football teams at three universities in Tokyo.[[209]](#cite_note-Friday_night_lights:_American_football_in_Japan_a_high_school_hit-211) Play was halted during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") by order of [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") [Hirohito](/wiki/Hirohito "Hirohito"), but the sport began growing in popularity again after the war.[[210]](#cite_note-On_an_Impulse:_Josh_Cox’s_journey_to_success_in_Japan’s_X-League-212) As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_football&action=edit), there are more than 400 high school football teams in Japan, with over 15,000 participants, and over 100 teams play in the Kantoh Collegiate Football Association (KCFA).[[209]](#cite_note-Friday_night_lights:_American_football_in_Japan_a_high_school_hit-211) The [X-League](/wiki/X-League_(Japan) "X-League (Japan)") is the largest American football league in Japan, and the largest American football league in the world to use a promotion-relegation system. Some teams in the X-League, like the [Panasonic Impulse](/wiki/Panasonic_Impulse "Panasonic Impulse"), are sponsored by corporations, and all Japanese players on these teams are employed by the corporation. The league operates in separate spring and fall seasons, with each team playing five games. The top eight teams make the playoffs, which are played in [Tokyo Dome](/wiki/Tokyo_Dome "Tokyo Dome"); the champion is determined by the [Rice Bowl](/wiki/Rice_Bowl "Rice Bowl").[[210]](#cite_note-On_an_Impulse:_Josh_Cox’s_journey_to_success_in_Japan’s_X-League-212)

Europe is a major target for the expansion of the game by football organizers. In the United Kingdom in the 1980s, the sport was popular, with the 1986 Super Bowl being watched by over four million people (about 1 out of every 14 Britons). Its popularity faded during the 1990s, coinciding with the establishment of the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") — the top level of the [English association football (soccer) league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"). According to [BBC America](/wiki/BBC_America "BBC America"), there is a "social stigma" surrounding American football in the UK, with many Brits feeling the sport has no right to call itself "football" due to the lack of emphasis on kicking.[[211]](#cite_note-Touchdown_in_the_U.K.:_Britain's_Long-Distance_Affair_with_the_NFL-213) Nonetheless, the sport has retained a following in the United Kingdom; the NFL operates a media network in the country, and since 2007 has hosted the [NFL International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series") in London. Super Bowl viewership has also rebounded, with over 4.4 million Britons watching [Super Bowl XLVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVI "Super Bowl XLVI").[[212]](#cite_note-Super_Bowl_caps_UK's_growing_gridiron_fever-214) The sport is played in European countries like Switzerland, which has American football clubs in every major city,[[213]](#cite_note-The_Game_Is_American,_but_the_View,_Alpine-215) and Germany, where the sport has around 45,000 registered amateur players.[[207]](#cite_note-American_football_touches_down_in_Germany-209)

In [Brazil](/wiki/American_football_in_Brazil "American football in Brazil"), football is a growing sport. It was generally unknown there until the 1980s when a small group of players began playing on [Copacabana Beach](/wiki/Copacabana_Beach "Copacabana Beach") in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"). The sport grew gradually with 700 amateur players registering within 20 years. Games were played on the beach with modified rules and without the traditional football equipment due to its lack of availability in Brazil. Eventually, a tournament, the Carioca championship, was founded, with the championship [Carioca Bowl](/wiki/Carioca_Bowl "Carioca Bowl") played to determine a league champion. The country saw its first full-pad game of football in October 2008.[[214]](#cite_note-216) According to *[The Rio Times](/wiki/The_Rio_Times "The Rio Times")*, the sport is one of the fastest-growing sports in Brazil and is almost as commonly played as soccer on the beaches of Copacabana and [Botafogo](/wiki/Botafogo "Botafogo").[[215]](#cite_note-Rio-217)

Football in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") is governed by the [Confederação Brasileira de Futebol Americano](/wiki/Confedera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Brasileira_de_Futebol_Americano "Confederação Brasileira de Futebol Americano") (CBFA), which had over 5,000 registered players as of November 2013. The sport's increase in popularity has been attributed to games aired on [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN"), which began airing in Brazil in 1992 with Portuguese commentary.[[216]](#cite_note-218) The popularity and "easy accessibility" of non-contact versions of the sport in Brazil has led to a rise in participation by female players.[[215]](#cite_note-Rio-217) According to ESPN, the American football audience in Brazil increased 800% between 2013 and 2016. The network, along with [Esporte Interativo](/wiki/Esporte_Interativo "Esporte Interativo"), airs games there on cable television. NFL had expressed interest in having games in the country, eventually resulting in São Paulo's [Arena Corinthians](/wiki/Arena_Corinthians "Arena Corinthians") hosting Brazil's first NFL game in 2024,[[217]](#cite_note-219) and the Super Bowl has become a widely watched event in Brazil at bars and [movie theaters](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater").[[218]](#cite_note-Crescimento_do_interesse_por_futebol_americano_no_Brasil_atrai_NFL-220)

Further countries have also expressed interest in football to a lesser degree. The [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") has expressed growing interest in American football, with many countries in the region being members of [IFAF Asia](/wiki/IFAF_Asia "IFAF Asia"). [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") and the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"), which are not members of IFAF, have established their own domestic leagues. [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") established two leagues for the sport, namely the Egyptian League of American Football and the [Egyptian Federation](/wiki/Egyptian_Federation_of_American_Football "Egyptian Federation of American Football") of American Football, and [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") hosts two teams based out of [Jeddah](/wiki/Jeddah "Jeddah") and [Yanbu](/wiki/Yanbu "Yanbu") respectively.[[219]](#cite_note-221)[[220]](#cite_note-222) [China](/wiki/China "China") has additionally been a target for the expansion of the sport, with the Mainland being the home of the [Chinese National Football League](/wiki/Chinese_National_Football_League "Chinese National Football League") as well as a growing audience of Super Bowl watchers. Three franchises are also based out of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), which prior to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") regularly played mainland teams. NFL games average 900,000 viewers in China, though the league has cited logistical challenges which would prevent teams from playing games akin to abroad games in European countries.[[221]](#cite_note-223)

## Related sports and variations

### Canadian football

[![Photograph of men playing flag football](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/US_Navy_111202-N-FC065-001_Cmdr._Bill_Mallory_tries_for_more_yards_after_a_reception_during_a_flag_football_game_celebrating_the_annual_Army-Navy_f.jpg/250px-US_Navy_111202-N-FC065-001_Cmdr._Bill_Mallory_tries_for_more_yards_after_a_reception_during_a_flag_football_game_celebrating_the_annual_Army-Navy_f.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_111202-N-FC065-001_Cmdr._Bill_Mallory_tries_for_more_yards_after_a_reception_during_a_flag_football_game_celebrating_the_annual_Army-Navy_f.jpg)

Men playing a game of [flag football](/wiki/Flag_football "Flag football") at the [Naval War College](/wiki/Naval_War_College "Naval War College") in [Newport, Rhode Island](/wiki/Newport,_Rhode_Island "Newport, Rhode Island")

[Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football"), the predominant form of football in Canada, is closely related to American football—both sports developed from rugby and are considered to be the chief variants of gridiron football.[[222]](#cite_note-Encyclopædia_Britannica-224) Although both games share a [similar set of rules](/wiki/Comparison_of_American_and_Canadian_football "Comparison of American and Canadian football"), there are several key rule differences: for example, in Canadian football the field measures 150 by 65 yards (137 by 59 m), including two 20-yard end zones (for a distance between goal lines of 110 yards),[[223]](#cite_note-Argos,_Rogers_Centre_agree_on_lease_deal_through_2017-225) teams have three downs instead of four, there are twelve players on each side instead of eleven,[[224]](#cite_note-Behind_IDFFL,_Canadian_players_chase_football_dreams-226) fair catches are not allowed, and a [rouge](/wiki/Single_(football) "Single (football)"), worth a single point is scored if the offense team kicks the ball out of the defense's end zone.[[225]](#cite_note-Head_to_Head:_CFL_vs._NFL-227) The Canadian Football League (CFL) is the major Canadian league and is the second-most popular sporting league in Canada, behind the National Hockey League.[[225]](#cite_note-Head_to_Head:_CFL_vs._NFL-227) [Football Canada](/wiki/Football_Canada "Football Canada") is the governing body for amateur Canadian football, and is also member of the International Federation of American Football.[[226]](#cite_note-Canadian_football_rules_recognized_by_IFAF-228)

NFL players have played in the CFL[[227]](#cite_note-Six_Ravens_Land_on_CFL_Negotiation_Lists-229) and vice versa;[[228]](#cite_note-NFL_stars_with_ties_to_the_CFL-230) quarterback [Warren Moon](/wiki/Warren_Moon "Warren Moon") is the only player in both [Canadian Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Canadian_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Canadian Football Hall of Fame") and the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame").[[229]](#cite_note-Gold_Jacket_Spotlight:_Warren_Moon_called_‘a_winner_on_and_off_the_field’-231) The NFL and CFL had a formal working relationship from 1997 to 2006.[[230]](#cite_note-232) The CFL has a strategic partnership with two American football leagues, the German Football League (GFL) and the Liga de Futbol Americano Profesional (LFA).[[231]](#cite_note-233) Rule changes announced in 2025 by the CFL will bring some elements of the sport closer to those of American football, while retaining the larger number of players, wider field, and three-down limit.[[232]](#cite_note-‘It’s_stood_the_test_of_time’:_CFL_players,_coaches_weigh_in_on_rule_changes-234)[[233]](#cite_note-U_Sports,_Football_Canada_both_taking_time_to_examine_CFL’s_proposed_changes-235)

### Variations

[Indoor football](/wiki/Indoor_American_football "Indoor American football") leagues constitute what *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* writer Mike Tanier described as the "most minor of minor leagues." Leagues are unstable, with franchises regularly moving from one league to another or merging with other teams, and teams or entire leagues dissolving completely; games are only attended by a small number of fans, and most players are [semi-professional](/wiki/Semi-professional "Semi-professional") athletes. The [Indoor Football League](/wiki/Indoor_Football_League "Indoor Football League") is an example of a prominent indoor league.[[234]](#cite_note-Staying_in_the_Game_on_Football's_Fringe-236) The [Arena Football League](/wiki/Arena_Football_League "Arena Football League"), which was founded in 1987 and ceased operations in 2019, was one of the longest-lived indoor football leagues.[[235]](#cite_note-GleesonChap7-237) In 2004, the league was called "America's fifth major sport" by *[ESPN The Magazine](/wiki/ESPN_The_Magazine "ESPN The Magazine")*.[[236]](#cite_note-Arena_football:_Is_it_America's_fifth_major_sport?-238)

Some schools that lack the enrollment numbers to run a competitive 11-man team choose to play [nine-man](/wiki/Nine-man_football "Nine-man football"), [eight-man](/wiki/Eight-man_football "Eight-man football"), or [six-man football](/wiki/Six-man_football "Six-man football"), which utilize modified rules to account for smaller team sizes.[[237]](#cite_note-What_is_9-man_football?_The_game_bringing_football_to_more_of_Alaska’s_youth-239)[[238]](#cite_note-Area_teams_making_transition_to_9-man_football-240)[[239]](#cite_note-The_X’s_and_O's_of_eight-man_football-241)[[240]](#cite_note-Six-man_football_is_more_than_touchdowns_and_wins_in_Texas:_'It's_like_an_identity'-242) Another variation played at some universities, [sprint football](/wiki/Sprint_football "Sprint football"), limits the maximum weight of players;[[241]](#cite_note-243) as of 2022, the maximum weight allowed is 178 pounds (81 kg) [[242]](#cite_note-Bellarmine_is_playing_sprint_football._Here's_what_you_need_to_know_about_the_sport-244)

There are several non-contact variants of football, such as [flag football](/wiki/Flag_football "Flag football").[[243]](#cite_note-NFL_FLAG_football_is_about_fun_and_fundamentals-245) In flag football the ball-carrier is not tackled; instead, defenders aim to pull a flag tied around the ball-carrier's waist.[[244]](#cite_note-Proper_flag_pull_takes_fast_feet_and_discipline-246) Flag football has become increasingly popular due to its lower likelihood of head injuries and its openness to female athletes; according to the NFL, which promotes flag football through NFL Flag, roughly 25% of flag football players are women and girls.[[245]](#cite_note-Flag_football_is_skyrocketing_in_popularity_nationwide_—_and_it's_not_just_for_the_boys-247) Another variant, [touch football](/wiki/Touch_football_(American) "Touch football (American)"), simply requires the ball-carrier to be touched to be considered downed. Depending on the rules used, a game of touch football may require the ball-carrier be touched with either one or two hands to be considered downed.[[246]](#cite_note-The_32_Rules_of_Thanksgiving_Touch_Football-248) [Wheelchair football](/wiki/Wheelchair_football "Wheelchair football") is a [parasport](/wiki/Parasports "Parasports") adapting the rules of football for people with physical disabilities.[[247]](#cite_note-NFL_to_the_World:_How_Wheelchair_American_Football_is_chasing_Paralympic_and_Invictus_Games_dream_in_historic_time_for_the_sport-249) Although it uses touch rules instead of tackling, wheelchair football isn't a non-contact sport; participants engage in both "big hits" and "physical blocks".[[248]](#cite_note-USA_Wheelchair_Football_League_debuts_in_Arizona-250)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** The terms "[gridiron football](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football")"[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica_1-1) and "gridiron" are synonyms for American football.[[2]](#cite_note-Macmillan-2) They are also used in a broader sense that includes [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football"), a football code that is [very similar](/wiki/Comparison_of_American_and_Canadian_football "Comparison of American and Canadian football") to American football.[[3]](#cite_note-Britannica_2-3)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-50)** In the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"), the kicking team is allowed to bring a 12th man on the field to hold the kickoff. This can only be done during windy games, when the ball is unable to stay on the tee. The player can only hold the kickoff, and must leave the field after the ball is kicked.[[48]](#cite_note-NFL_teams_will_be_allowed_to_have_12_players_on_the_field_for_this_one_specific_situation_during_2024_season-49)
